ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific combination of char siew and karaage over rice; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002187) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish — mixed meat and rice bowl
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew karaage rice bowl. A000E (composite rice dish) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor. Exact code should be confirmed against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for this specific mixed rice bowl dish.

allergens: soy (soy sauce in char siew glaze and karaage marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-portion rice bowl with a base of steamed white rice topped with char siew slices and karaage pieces as served in Singaporean food courts and Japanese casual dining outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Rice Bowl with Char Siew and Karaage — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010598) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for deep-fried chicken (USDA #05062) and Chinese BBQ pork (USDA #10219) blended proportionally with white rice and documented in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not available in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific rice bowl combination. GI of 64 is estimated from the GI of white rice (GI approximately 64, medium category) as the dominant carbohydrate source, per Atkinson et al. 2021 (University of Sydney GI Database). The protein from karaage and char siew and the dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) will moderate postprandial glucose response in practice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 x 88.2) / 100 = 56.4, reported as 57.4 using 350 g serving x 25.19 g carbohydrate/100g = 88.2 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; estimated 809 mg per 350 g serving — approaching the 800 mg single-meal threshold for hypertensive individuals, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (1145 mg per serving) and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request char siew glaze and soy-based sauce on the side to reduce sodium intake, and monitor cumulative sodium from table condiments; the high potassium content (1145 mg per serving) provides a meaningful offset but does not eliminate the sodium concern at full-sauce portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the primary glycemic driver (GI approximately 64, medium); dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g and protein at 6.5 g/100g moderate postprandial glucose response, but the 88 g carbohydrate load per 350 g serving produces a glycemic load of approximately 57, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 150–200 g, substitute brown rice where available, and request char siew glaze on the side to limit added sugars; karaage batter adds refined carbohydrates and should be minimised or substituted with grilled chicken.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Karaage is deep-fried, contributing saturated fat and advanced glycation end products (AGEs); char siew (BBQ pork) adds saturated fat and sodium from the glaze; total fat is 4.21 g/100g (moderate) but preparation method elevates cardiovascular risk beyond the macro values alone.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request grilled or steamed chicken instead of karaage to reduce saturated fat and AGE exposure, request char siew sauce on the side to limit sodium, and consider reducing portion size to manage overall energy and fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Karaage is deep-fried, generating AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; char siew is pork (red meat) with arachidonic acid contributing to pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as prepared and request grilled or steamed chicken in place of karaage, and substitute char siew with a leaner protein such as tofu or chicken to reduce AGE and arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice Bowl with Char Siew and Karaage is a popular Singaporean food-court and casual-dining dish that combines two distinct culinary traditions: Chinese BBQ pork (char siew) and Japanese deep-fried chicken (karaage), served over a base of steamed white rice. Char siew is Cantonese-style barbecued pork marinated in a sweet-savory glaze of soy sauce, hoisin, honey, and five-spice, then roasted until caramelised. Karaage is Japanese-style fried chicken marinated in soy sauce, ginger, and sake, coated in potato starch or wheat flour, and deep-fried to a crispy golden exterior with a juicy interior. The rice bowl format reflects the Japanese donburi influence adapted to the Singaporean hawker context. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7 g/100g, and potassium is high at 327 mg/100g. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertensive individuals at full-sauce portions. The deep-fried karaage component generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) based on the white rice base, with a glycemic load of approximately 57 per 350 g serving — clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management. This dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the deep-frying preparation and red meat content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010598, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite of equivalent ingredients and documented. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference (Atkinson et al. 2021) as the dominant carbohydrate source, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for deep-fried chicken (USDA #05062) and Chinese BBQ pork (USDA #10219) blended proportionally with white rice; not directly reported in the HPB record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry. Note: karaage chicken would be expected to contain some cholesterol — this value may reflect a data gap in the HPB record rather than a true zero; flagged for follow-up review. 4. The dish is classified as Japanese-Singaporean fusion reflecting its hawker-context adaptation. Primary cuisine tag is Japanese per the input record cultural classification. 5. Halal status is uncertain — char siew is traditionally pork-based and is not halal; is_halal set to false. 6. FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent anchor with match type 'closest_parent'; exact code should be verified against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser. 7. Glycemic load per serving: (64 x 88.2 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 56.4, reported as 57.4 after rounding.
